data.title: China Overseas Mining 2025 - Gold And Energy Transition Metals
data.description:
In December 1988, the Channar iron ore mine in Western Australia, then owned jointly by Rio Tinto Group (RIO) and Sinosteel Corp., embarked on its first stage of development. The project involved international financing and marked China's inaugural overseas mining investment. A decade later, in 1998, China completed its first overseas nonferrous mine transaction when China Nonferrous Mining Corp. Ltd. acquired the Chambishi copper mine in Zambia. Today, propelled by the "Go Out" policy, an increasing number of Chinese companies are actively exploring international opportunities. Those that have achieved success are further expanding their global presence. This investment strategy has significantly enhanced China's attributable production of gold and key energy transition metals.

Africa, Latin America focus of China's overseas M&A

A 2021 study identified a growing emphasis on China's mining M&A in Africa and Latin America, particularly during the 2010s. Data from 2020 onward further corroborates this trend, with Chinese companies' M&A activities in Africa and Latin America comprising 49% and 30%, respectively, of their $14.6 billion of total overseas M&A spending till 2025 year to date. This indicates a deepening presence in these regions. Moreover, the M&A data highlights a strong interest in gold and key energy transition metals, with gold, copper and lithium accounting for 86% of the transactions.

Rising gold investments

Since 2020, China's overseas M&A spending has consistently surpassed $2 billion a year. However, after China Ex-work battery lithium carbonate prices dropped almost 90% from the peak in November 2022 at $79,650/t, the focus has shifted. Overseas lithium trades slowed after 2021, while gold investments surged in 2024, culminating in six deals worth $1.7 billion.

Among major Chinese mining companies, Zijin Mining Group (OTCPK:ZIJMF)(OTCPK:ZIJMY) leads in overseas investment, contributing approximately 28% of the total M&A spending since 2020. As a pioneer in global expansion, Zijin Mining has adhered to its principle of "leveraging world-class projects and prioritizing resources. "Although rising gold prices have also pushed asset values up and raised the threshold for M&A, Zijin Mining continues its overseas ventures, completing significant transactions in Peru, Ghana and Kazakhstan. Shandong Gold Mining Co. Ltd. (OTC:SDGMF) and Zhaojin Mining Industry Co. Ltd. are also actively acquiring stakes in gold assets. As the emphasis on critical minerals increases, noncritical minerals, such as gold, continue to present promising opportunities. Our latest Gold Commodity Briefing Service expects a continued bull run of gold prices in 2026, with a projection of $3,897 per ounce.

Copper production success

China's overseas copper investments have yielded substantial results. The country's attributable share of global mined copper production increased from 10% in 2016 to 19% in 2024, while its overseas copper production surged 369% during the same period, reaching approximately 2.6 million metric tons and surpassing domestic output. According to our Copper Commodity Briefing Service, while China's domestic mined copper production accounts for only 8% of the world's total, its smelter-grade copper output represents 48%. This disparity highlights China's reliance on overseas copper resources to satisfy its robust demand. China Nonferrous Mining was among the first to explore overseas copper resources, paving the way for other companies, such as CMOC Group (OTCPK:CMCLF) and Zijin Mining, to join the race for copper mining.

Increased attributable production has enabled Chinese companies to achieve higher revenue. CMOC's Tenke Fungurume and Kisanfu mines have significantly boosted production; the two operations combined currently yield 600,000 metric tons per year of copper. The company plans to advance its expansion, with preliminary exploration underway at Tenke Fungurume West and Kisanfu Phase II. Meanwhile, the Kamoa-Kakula mine's third concentrator commissioning in May 2024 has driven Zijin Mining's copper growth. Kamoa Copper SA, a joint venture between Ivanhoe Mines Ltd. and Zijin Mining, expects copper production at the Kamoa-Kakula mine to reach 400,000 metric tons in 2025, despite the mine experiencing underground seismic activity.

The success stories of CMOC and Zijin Mining are likely to inspire more Chinese companies to pursue world-class copper assets. Using the company-level cost curve, companies whose equity production in 2024 was at least 100,000 metric tons higher than in 2020 have been identified. We have also outlined these companies' 2020 production and all-in-sustaining margin with dotted boxes in the figure below, with these two major companies demonstrating significant production growth and increased unit profits.

China's lithium refining advantage

The conversion of lithium concentrate into lithium hydroxide or lithium carbonate is crucial for battery production. As of Sept. 23, 2025, the difference between the Platts lithium spodumene 5.5% concentrate CIF China price and the lithium hydroxide delivered duty paid (DDP) China price — on a lithium carbonate equivalent (LCE) basis — stood at $3,610 per metric ton. Platts is part of S&P Global Commodity Insights. This indicates that lithium hydroxide producers must manage their concentrate conversion costs below this threshold to maintain profitability. Outside China, few processing plants have managed to keep refining costs under $4,000/t. For instance, IGO Ltd.'s Kwinana plant reduced its unit refining costs significantly within a year by the June quarter of 2025, but it still exceeds $10,000/t. In contrast, due to fierce domestic competition, maintaining unit conversion costs below $3,000/t is normal in China. This cost efficiency has become a lifeline, ensuring China's acquisition of overseas hard rock lithium mines remains economically viable. In 2024, China's attributable lithium production ranked highest globally, accounting for 33.4% of the global total. Notably, its overseas output reached 238,844 metric tons LCE, with six hard rock lithium mines in Africa acquired and commissioned after 2021.

Continued global mining journey

China's overseas mining ventures are expected to continue, evidenced by its nickel investments in Indonesia following the 2020 nickel ore export ban. Chinese companies have expanded smelting capacity in Indonesia, demonstrating that even with policy restrictions, China's substantial demand will continue to drive its pursuit of overseas mineral resources.

China has gained an advantage in the supply side of energy transition minerals such as copper and lithium, benefiting from years of consistent investment. As more Chinese companies engage in global ventures, they are also investing in bauxite, graphite, manganese and other minerals. This diversification indicates that China's global mineral resource development will continue to grow, potentially leading to even greater stories of expansion in the future.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Don't Waste Your Time Timing Bubbles: Subscriptions Make This Market Nothing Like 1999
data.description:
Subscriptions are the new taxes for the S&P 500

Boy oh boy, my podcast and media content feeds have turned the market's (NYSEARCA:SPY)(NYSEARCA:DIA)(NYSEARCA:IVV)(NYSEARCA:VOO)(NASDAQ:QQQ) bearish bubble dial up to 99. It's fair to make a lot of comparisons to 1999; we're trading along the same CAPE ratio trends, price to sales, and price to cash flow, all of which have similarities. Earnings growth is quite a bit different, however, as is earnings resiliency.

In the year 2000, Exxon Mobil (XOM) was the top dog, while Cisco (CSCO), Microsoft (MSFT), and Intel (INTC) were the only tech companies in the top 10 at the time, and only one SaaS company. Not much in the way of subscription-based revenue. The rest were quite cyclical in nature. Now let's take a look at 2025:

70%, or 7 out of 10, of the top 10 companies are tech-related and all either have or have the goal of implementing subscription-based models. Even if we were to dig into Walmart (WMT) or Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B)(BRK.A), I'm sure you will find subscription based models [Walmart+, utilities, and insurance for Berkshire]. With the advent and growth of credit cards and autopay, subscription based models are not only sticky, they are getting to be as reliable as rent or taxes.

Ask yourself this question: in 1999, how many things did you have set up with autopay? How many things did you mail in a check for? I am betting the latter [mailing in a check] was the more common payment method of the two at that time. Breaking the relationship was much easier. Now, checks are not worth the paper they're printed on and are nearly obsolete. Lines of credit make for instant access to your balance sheet by these companies unless you either lose your creditworthiness or take the time out of your tired, post 10 hour work-shift evening to cancel your recurring subscription because you found something else. There has never been a better time to be a company running a subscription based model; everyone wants in.

Unsurprisingly, credit card payments are expected to rise pretty consistently over the next decade. As more consumers have credit, companies will have more and more instant access to your consumer balance sheet.

The market follows earnings

The market follows earnings. Most of us get our market exposure via ETFs or mutual funds that are modeled after the S&P 500 (SP500). We can see when charting the level of the S&P 500 index with the earnings per share growth of the index, the two follow each other both in terms of upswings and downswings. If earnings recede for long periods, the stock market crumbles. If earnings keep growing, the market tends to stay resilient.

A list of 100 companies with subscription models in the S&P 500

Company Ticker Subscription Model examples
1 Microsoft (MSFT) Microsoft 365, Azure
2 Adobe (ADBE) Creative Cloud
3 Salesforce (CRM) Cloud CRM licenses
4 ServiceNow (NOW) IT workflow SaaS
5 Workday (WDAY) HR & ERP SaaS
6 Intuit (INTU) QuickBooks / TurboTax
7 Autodesk (ADSK) CAD SaaS licenses
8 Synopsys (SNPS) EDA software subscriptions
9 Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) Design software licensing
10 Ansys (ANSS) Engineering simulation subscriptions
11 Oracle (ORCL) Cloud and software licensing
12 IBM (IBM) Cloud & software subscriptions
13 Cisco (CSCO) Networking software subscriptions
14 Akamai (AKAM) CDN & security service
15 Automatic Data Processing (ADP) Payroll SaaS subscriptions
16 Paychex (PAYX) Payroll SaaS subscriptions
17 Palo Alto Networks (PANW) Cybersecurity
18 CrowdStrike (CRWD) Endpoint protection SaaS
19 Fortinet (FTNT) Security software
20 F5 (FFIV) Network software subscriptions
21 Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) GreenLake “as-a-service” model
22 HP Inc. (HPQ) Instant Ink / HP+
23 Broadcom (AVGO) Enterprise software subscriptions
24 Verisign (VRSN) Domain registry subscriptions
25 Netflix (NFLX) Streaming subscriptions
26 Walt Disney (DIS) Disney+, ESPN+, Hulu
27 Amazon (AMZN) Prime membership
28 Apple (AAPL) iCloud, Music, TV+
29 Alphabet (GOOGL) Google One, Workspace
30 Uber (UBER) Uber One
31 Best Buy (BBY) My Best Buy Memberships
32 Walmart (WMT) Walmart+
33 Costco (COST) Membership fees
34 Kroger (KR) Boost by Kroger Plus
35 Fox (FOXA) Fox Nation subscription
36 AT&T (T) Wireless & broadband plans
37 Verizon (VZ) Wireless & internet subscriptions
38 T-Mobile US (TMUS) Wireless service subscriptions
39 Comcast (CMCSA) Internet, TV, Peacock
40 Charter Communications (CHTR) Spectrum subscriptions
41 S&P Global (SPGI) Data subscriptions
42 Moody’s (MCO) Rating & data subscriptions
43 MSCI (MSCI) Index licensing
44 FactSet (FDS) Financial data subscriptions
45 Verisk Analytics (VRSK) Data analytics subscriptions
46 Nasdaq (NDAQ) Market data subscriptions
47 Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) Exchange data & clearing
48 CME Group (CME) Exchange data subscriptions
49 Cboe Global Markets (CBOE) Market data subscriptions
50 Gartner (IT) Research subscriptions
51 Equinix (EQIX) Data center colocation contracts
52 Digital Realty (DLR) Data center recurring leases
53 American Tower (AMT) Tower leases
54 Iron Mountain (IRM) Storage & data subscriptions
55 Public Storage (PSA) Monthly storage contracts
56 Extra Space Storage (EXR) Monthly storage contracts
57 Waste Management (WM) Waste collection contracts
58 Republic Services (RSG) Waste service subscriptions
59 Waste Connections (WCN) Waste & recycling contracts
60 American Water Works (AWK) Monthly billing (utilities)
61 Essential Utilities (WTRG) Water service subscriptions
62 UnitedHealth Group (UNH) Health insurance premiums
63 Elevance Health (ELV) Health insurance premiums
64 Cigna (CI) Health insurance premiums
65 Humana (HUM) Health insurance premiums
66 CVS Health (CVS) Aetna & CarePass
67 Centene (CNC) Managed care premiums
68 Allstate (ALL) Auto & home insurance
69 Progressive (PGR) Auto insurance
70 Travelers (TRV) Property & casualty insurance
71 Chubb (CB) Global insurance
72 AIG (AIG) P&C and life insurance
73 MetLife (MET) Life insurance
74 Prudential Financial (PRU) Life insurance
75 The Hartford (HIG) Commercial insurance
76 Aflac (AFL) Supplemental insurance
77 Marsh & McLennan (MMC) Ongoing risk services
78 Willis Towers Watson (WTW) Advisory & risk subscriptions
79 NextEra Energy (NEE) Monthly electricity service
80 Duke Energy (DUK) Monthly electricity service
81 Southern Company (SO) Monthly electricity service
82 Dominion Energy (D) Monthly electricity service
83 American Electric Power (AEP) Monthly electricity service
84 Exelon (EXC) Monthly electricity service
85 Constellation Energy (CEG) Monthly electricity service
86 Sempra (SRE) Utility billing
87 Xcel Energy (XEL) Utility billing
88 FirstEnergy (FE) Utility billing
89 Entergy (ETR) Utility billing
90 Edison International (EIX) Utility billing
91 Public Service Enterprise Group (PEG) Utility billing
92 WEC Energy Group (WEC) Utility billing
93 CMS Energy (CMS) Utility billing
94 NiSource (NI) Utility billing
95 Ameren (AEE) Utility billing
96 Alliant Energy (LNT) Utility billing
97 NRG Energy (NRG) Energy plans
98 DTE Energy (DTE) Utility billing
99 Pinnacle West Capital (PNW) Utility billing
100 PPL Corp (PPL) Utility billing

This is just 100; you can probably think of more

This is just a list of 100 of the S&P 500 that came to mind, there's more, but this is just a nice round number. While within the earnings of each of these companies, 100% is not subscription based, but all are forever trying to grow that subscription based revenue and income to become a bigger and bigger part of what's incoming.

Brick and mortar is left out, but they are also left out of the S&P 500

For those companies that have no e-commerce and no subscription based revenue in their models, there are certainly many that you can think of. I would also argue that many of those companies have no place in any of the major benchmark indexes. If you are invested in the S&P 500, Nasdaq, or Dow 30 through index or mutual funds, you are substantially invested in a stream of subscription based revenue models.

This is not the 1999 risk free rate

I also made the point in a separate market article that the risk free rate is not similar to that of 1999. Let's observe:

First, the risk free rate, as measured by the US Ten Year Treasury, was yielding in excess of 6% for much of the late 1990s. The market, on the other hand, was trading in the 30X + P/E ratio range [3.3% earnings yield] at the same time:

Currently, we have rates around 4.2% and likely to fall into next year as the Fed Chair and board of governors get a shake up. Meanwhile, the forward P/E ratio of 23X is about a 4.2% earnings yield, in line with the risk free rate. The alternative to stocks [the US 10 year] is nowhere near as attractive now as it was in 1999, and the earnings growth of these companies is becoming more dependable with more revenue coming from recurring consumer payments.

How can you stop these balance sheets?

I also made the counterpoint in the same article that the balance sheets for most of big tech have not even been tapped yet. The argument that CAPEX AI spending has to collapse any day now fails to take into consideration that these are self funding companies that expand based on their own free cash flow. Most have borrowed almost nothing compared to their EBITDA. If you're calling for CAPEX spending to dry up, I think you're early.

We used to be a platoon; now we're an army

Then we get to financial writers and pundits like myself. In 1999 we had Jim Cramer; I can't remember many others, but I was too young to be paying much attention. Now YouTube, X.com, Instagram, TikTok, Seeking Alpha, etc., are stacked with financial influencers and those sharing information on what they believe is a good buy. You also have the institutions like Schwab and Fidelity that produce reports on retirement and how much you actually need to retire. They always have you looking over your shoulder and feeling guilty if you are not stacking your 401K.

In short, financial information and investment analysis are in information overload mode and will only grow with time. If you want to remain a bear for a decade, good luck avoiding the bulls telling you to buy the dip or the bottom unless you are a monk and retreat to the Shaolin temple to learn how to punch holes in pine trees. Even then, I've observed the monks around Shaolin playing with their cell phones when I visited.

A cashless society makes this model easier and easier to tap

Also there is no denying that society is getting closer and closer to becoming cashless. Whether you use debit or credit, the chances are more and more of your payments are digital and subscription based because the least amount of friction is to set it and forget it. As nearly every payment becomes digital, tapping into the autopay model in a consumer's life is ever more easy to accomplish for these corporations. Companies are getting access to consumer cash flows with bond like consistency.

Summary

It's hard to still be a bull in the face of all the bears that are out there. I'm not saying we are in a cheap pricing territory, but we are also not in cash out your retirement and put it under the mattress land. We could easily see a 10-20% pullback based on a black swan of some sort or prolonged job losses. My point is I don't think it lasts very long, and it will be hard to get back in after cashing out. Staying the course and having faith that earnings growth has become more resilient than ever due to recurring revenue models and less friction to auto payments is something for capitalists to celebrate. Cash reserves are your best hedge to ride out volatility until earnings grow into their valuations.

data.title: Immatics: Multiple Clinical Catalysts In Q4'25 And 2026
data.description:
Immatics N.V. (NASDAQ:IMTX) is developing a cell therapy called Anzu-cel (anzutresgene autoleucel, IMA203) for melanoma, and other potential cancers, that could bring in hundreds of millions or more annually. The company has already produced some data with Anzu-cel and is now running a large head-to-head study in melanoma which will produce interim and final data in 2026. The company also has three other therapies in the clinic, with additional data expected in Q4'25. This article looks at where the company is now, and makes some comparisons to an already approved competing product Amtagvi (lifileucel) from Iovance Biotherapeutics (IOVA).

Anzu-cel

Anzu-cel (anzutresgene autoleucel, IMA203) is IMTX's most advanced candidate and is made producing a T-cell receptor (TCR) T-cell therapy from a patient's own cells. That T-cell is engineered to target cancer cells that express PRAME (a cancer associated protein), or a fragment thereof, when loaded onto a particular HLA complex (HLA-A*02:01). Unfortunately, the prevalence of HLA-A*02:01 is only 41% of people in the US and 48% in Europe, according to estimates supplied by IMTX, so those without this allele are not candidates for treatment with Anzu-cel.

Nonetheless, with potential in multiple cancers, and a potential addressable market of nearly 9,000 patients per year in the US & EU5 in melanoma alone, there is still substantial money to be made. For example, were the therapy to launch at a price of $500K, and IMTX captured just 1000 melanoma patients per annum, this would correspond to $500M in revenue. It should be noted that competitor Iovance (IOVA) launched its cell therapy for melanoma, Amtagvi (lifileucel) at a price of $515K. That being said, IOVA has struggled to make it towards 500 patients treated in 2025, infusing 102 patients in Q2'25, despite that representing the fourth full quarter of Amtagvi's launch, and the therapy not requiring patients to have a particular allele.

The comparison between IMTX's Anzu-cel, and IOVA's Amtagvi, is particularly relevant, given Amtagvi is a few years ahead and a competitor in melanoma, but also other tumors. Notably, Anzu-cel doesn't require a surgery to harvest tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes like Amtagvi, instead cells can be harvested from the patient's blood via leukapheresis. Further, the turnaround time on generating Anzu-cel following that is about 14 days, compared to over 30 for IOVA's Amtagvi. Lastly, there isn't a need for high-dose interleukin-2 following infusion of Anzu-cel, whereas patients treated with Amtagvi do receive high-dose IL-2. Of course IOVA is developing next generation products, and there are other competitors out there, but there are some clear advantages to Anzu-cel over Amtagvi, at least in the 40-50% of melanoma patients that would be eligible for Anzu-cel.

As for data generated to date, the company provided an update from its phase 1b work in mid-2025, noting a 56% confirmed objective response rate (ORR) in 32 melanoma patients, and a 91% disease control rate. Notably, there was efficacy in the difficult to treat uveal melanoma (10 from 15 patients achieving confirmed objective responses).

Moving forward, the company has additional updates from its phase 1b work coming at the European Society for Medical Oncology (ESMO) Congress 2025 on October 20. The company will also continue to use that study to look at uveal melanoma patients, enrolling another 30 beginning in Q4'25.

Of higher impact will be the results from a phase 3 trial of Anzu-cel in cutaneous melanoma called SUPRAME. IMTX began the 360-patient SUPRAME trial in December 2024, but with enrolment at 65 sites in the US and Europe, the trial is already expected to reach an interim and final analysis in 2026 (dependent on event rates).

The design of SUPRAME is a strength. In an era where Replimune (REPL) found its single-arm Ignyte study in melanoma could not satisfy what seems to be a tougher regulator, IMTX has the chance to show its therapy is a better choice than other options in second-line-and-beyond (2L+) melanoma. Of course REPL might yet convince the regulator otherwise, or run additional studies, and thus still represents a potential IMTX competitor.

I believe IMTX has a good chance of showing Anzu-cel is a superior option to the others with SUPRAME, especially since many patients in the control arm probably won't access lifileucel as they are in Europe. Even for patients enrolled in the US, their physician might try something else first.

Beyond Anzu-cel

IMTX has other therapeutics in the clinic, including IMA203CD8 (a second generation PRAME-targeting cell therapy), IMA402 and IMA401 (two bispecific T-cell engagers).

IMA203CD8

For IMA203CD8, an update is expected in Q4'25, the company has already reported some data from phase 1a dose escalation work, where there were some dose-limiting toxicities seen at dose level 4b. Nonetheless, responses to therapy were seen, not just in melanoma, but also in synovial sarcoma, with the company reporting a 41% confirmed ORR (14 from 34 patients), at what it believes are sub-optimal doses.

Investors will be hoping then, that IMTX has got a hold on toxicity issues with IMA203CD8. Indeed as of Q1'25 earnings, the company noted infusing patients with up to eight billion cells (dose level 6) despite previously hitting issues at DL4b.

IMA402 and IMA401

IMA402 is a bispecific, antibody-like T-cell engager, that binds to CD3 and a fragment of PRAME loaded onto HLA-A*02:01. With Q1'25 earnings, IMTX noted it had moved through 11 dose levels in its dose-escalation work (n=33 patients) which allows enrolment of various solid tumor types (patients are most melanoma nonetheless). The company also noted a confirmed partial response in a melanoma patient at dose level 7.

Phase 1a dose escalation is ongoing, and the next update with clinical data at relevant dose levels with a focus on second-line or later (2L) melanoma is planned for 4Q 2025.

ITMX comments regarding IMA402. Q2'25 earnings press release.

With an update from the dose-escalation work expected in Q4'25, and the company now in the range of doses that seem to have better activity, I think IMTX has a good chance of impressing with updated IMA402 data this quarter. Importantly, since IMA402 is an antibody-like biologic, not a cell therapy, the concerns about launching a cell therapy (such as the issues IOVA has seen), would not apply. Indeed the company notes IMA402 can allow the company to expand into early-stage PRAME expressing cancers, not limited to, but including first-line cutaneous melanoma.

As for IMA401, it is another antibody-like T-cell engager, that binds CD3 and a peptide fragment from MAGEA4 or MAGEA8 loaded onto HLA-A*02:01. The company previously reported some data at ESMO 2024 noting it had treated 35 patients, with various solid tumor types, in its phase 1a dose-escalation trial. That data included an efficacy evaluable population of 29 patients, with six responses seen (21% ORR). Response rates were higher in those treated with relevant IMA401 doses and with high MAGEA4/8 levels. IMA401 has similar advantages to IMA402, it would be easier to scale up than launching a cell therapy and it is more amenable to moves into earlier stage cancers. IMTX acknowledges its primary goal is to develop IMA401 in earlier lines of treatment. A Q4'25 update will include more data from the phase 1a work, including in lung and head and neck cancer.

Financial Overview

IMTX reported cash, cash equivalents and other financial assets of €478.2M at the end of Q2'25 ($560.5M using an exchange rate of 1 Euro to 1.172 USD). For Q2'25, IMTX reported; collaboration revenue of €4.7M, R&D expenses of €45.1M, G&A expenses of €12.8M and a net loss of €70.3M (impacted partly by unrealized foreign exchange rate losses). For the first six months of 2025, IMTX reported net cash used in operating activities of €73.3M. At that rate of burn, IMTX's cash could last another six halves, or three years, thus into H2'28. Of course with the larger SUPRAME trial now running, and the company developing its other agents further, cash burn could pick up. Indeed IMTX is only guiding for cash to last into H2'27, although this is beyond the planned timeline for the interim and final analysis of SUPRAME.

IMTX currently trades with a market cap of $1.32B ($10.90 per share).

Risks

There are multiple risks to any long position in IMTX, a few of which are discussed in this section.

Firstly, IMTX's interim result from SUPRAME could disappoint, leading to a heavy pullback in the stock. IMTX could also pullback if there is disappointing data presented in Q4'25 from its other pipeline members.

Secondly, there can be delays in enrolling SUPRAME or delays in producing results from IMTX's other trials, which could see some traders/investors exit their position to come back later.

Lastly, IMTX might use its elevated market cap to raise further funds. A raise during a run up into SUPRAME results might be a way of the company making sure it has funds for years to come, but could hit the stock.

Conclusions and rating

IMTX has enjoyed the recent surge of the biotech sector, with the stock up 88% in the past month, perhaps aided by a run-up into Q4'25 updates from many of its trials. The bigger catalyst is the SUPRAME interim and final analysis in 2026, although I suppose it is possible the data from its other therapies steals the show in Q4'25. A particularly near-term catalyst would be more Anzu-cel data at ESMO 2025, where additional data, from uveal melanoma patients, will face the challenge of keeping up with the rate of response seen so far (recall 10/15 patients saw a confirmed response previously).

That being said, there are multiple shots on goal in Q4'25, with updates from the company's next gen IMA203CD8 cell therapy, IMA402 and IMA401. With each of those three pipeline candidates, the company has passed the initial stage where a lot of data is from lower, less effective, or not effective at all, dose levels. It is now time for each of those therapies to confirm the promise seen in earlier data, where those treated with higher doses appear to show greater efficacy. I think IMTX has a good chance of confirming just that in at least one of those three cases, I'd be surprised if none of IMA203CD8, IMA402 or IMA401 provided impressive response rates at higher doses.

Given those multiple shots on goal, and the fact that any pullback might be followed by additional runup into or in 2026 because of the SUPRAME interim, I now rate IMTX a buy. A further strength is the company having the cash to make it well beyond these readouts, although the company raising additional cash is always a possibility.

data.title: Granite Construction Is Deeply Undervalued (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
One of my best calls over the last couple of years now has been Granite Construction Incorporated (NYSE:GVA), a firm that plays in the construction industry through the provision of infrastructure solutions for public and private clients alike. Examples include, but are not limited to, site preparation, mining, and infrastructure services. But it also touches on the mass transit facility space, airports, bridges, railroads, the residential development space, energy, and more. In the last article that I wrote about the company, published in early July of this year, I marveled at the strong growth that the company had exhibited. Revenue, profits, and cash flows were all on the rise. Shares were not as cheap as I perhaps would have wanted them to be. But at the end of the day, I argued that the company was attractive enough to justify a ‘buy’ rating.

Since then, shares have appreciated by 15.5%, more than double the 7.2% rise that the S&P 500 exhibited. But that's nothing. Since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ in March of 2023, the stock is up 168.8%. That's almost triple the 63.3% increase that the broader market enjoyed. Today, I would argue that shares are not as cheap as they once were. However, with the company still trading well below other similar enterprises, I believe that it still deserves a very bullish rating. In fact, I would go so far as to say it's time to upgrade it to a ‘strong buy’.

This opportunity still has legs

From a purely fundamental standpoint, I don't think anybody would disagree with me when I say that Granite Construction is doing quite well. Take the most recent quarter as an example, which would be the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company came in at $1.13 billion. That's an increase of 4% over the $1.08 billion the company reported one year earlier. This rise in revenue came from both of its operating segments.

For starters, its Construction segment reported a jump in revenue from $918 million to $937.4 million. This increase came despite the fact that public funding declined, with revenue attributable to the company from that source dropping from $696.7 million to $651.9 million. But private funding more than made up for this, jumping from $221.2 million to $285.5 million thanks in part to the firm's acquisition of Dickerson & Bowen. This was an enterprise that management purchased in August of last year and exchanged for $125.5 million. For context, it operates as an aggregates, asphalt, and highway construction business that caters to the market in Mississippi. It was responsible for $22.7 million of revenue in the most recent quarter alone.

We also have the Materials segment of the company, which is really divided between the aggregates and the asphalt products that the business provides. Aggregates revenue grew from $54.3 million to $59.6 million. And asphalt revenue jumped from $109.4 million to $128.6 million. $5.4 million of the segment revenue increase came from the aforementioned acquisition. However, most of it was thanks to higher aggregates and asphalt volumes and higher average sales prices.

With revenue for the company improving, profitability also improved. Net income skyrocketed from $36.9 million to $71.7 million. But if we adjust for certain one-time events, such as a $27.8 million loss on the extinguishment of debt that the company incurred in the second quarter of last year, we would get a more modest increase from $77 million to $86.3 million. Operating cash flow went from negative $2 million to positive $1.8 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $96.7 million to $108.8 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $129.9 million to $152.4 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Once again, revenue, profits, and cash flows largely increased year over year. And if everything goes according to plan, investors should expect continued growth this year. Some of this might be attributable to organic growth, such as the improvement when it came to aggregates and asphalt which management claimed was because of higher prices and higher volumes. But some of it should come from acquisitions.

In early August, for instance, management announced the purchase of two different companies. These were none other than Warren Paving and Papich Construction. The first of these purchases, Warren Paving, brings into the fold a major aggregates producer that is vertically integrated in the Mississippi River and Gulf Coast regions. Its assets include a quarry, a sand and gravel operation, 11 aggregate yards, three asphalt plants, and a fleet of 168 owned and leased barges. All told, it will add over 400 million tons of aggregate reserves to the company's portfolio. It generates about $275 million of revenue and $52 million worth of EBITDA. As for Papich Construction, it serves as an infrastructure projects company that supplies its own internal projects and third-party customers with asphalt and aggregate products. This brings with it a gravel mine, two quarries, and two asphalt plants. This particular business generates around $150 million of revenue and should be responsible for approximately $24.5 million worth of EBITDA.

This particular set of acquisitions will cost shareholders $710 million. But at the end of the day, based on my own calculations shown above, they should bring in around $76.5 million of EBITDA, $35.2 million of operating cash flow, and $19.5 million of net income. This is based on a 5% interest rate on the debt that the company is taking in order to make this purchase happen, and it's based on applying reasonable depreciation, depletion, and amortization, as well as tax expenses, to the business.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management figured that revenue would come in at between $4.35 billion and $4.55 billion. With an EBITDA margin of between 11.25% and 12.25%, this should get us around $522.9 million of EBITDA for the year. Management pointed out that only about $150 million of the revenue generated this year will come from these aforementioned purchases. On a pro rata basis, that would mean EBITDA of approximately $27 million. Applying this, as well as pro rata figures for net income and operating cash flow to the equation, we would get EBITDA for this year of $572.8 million. Adjusted operating cash flow would be about $442.5 million, while adjusted net profits would be $296.9 million.

Using these figures, incorporating the new net debt into the equation, and making the same adjustments for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are multiples that would normally justify a more neutral assessment in my book. But I have always felt that the stability and simple business model that exist in this market justify higher multiples than what is typical. So honestly, I view these multiples as rather attractive. Furthermore, compared to other similar firms, shares are certainly cheap. In the table below, I stacked it up against five other similar enterprises. What I found was that Granite Construction was the cheapest across the board.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Granite Construction 16.2 10.8 10.3
MYR Group Inc. (MYRG) 42.2 18.7 17.6
Construction Partners, Inc. (ROAD) 91.2 24.7 26.6
Primoris Services Corporation (PRIM) 31.7 11.5 17.0
Sterling Infrastructure, Inc. (STRL) 36.7 21.1 25.8
Ameresco, Inc. (AMRC) 32.3 11.7 19.2

To illustrate just how much upside potential exists for this business, I created the chart below. In it, you can see how much upside the company would justify if it were to trade at the lowest of the multiples of the five companies I compared it to. I did the same thing, in the same chart, under the assumption that it should trade at the average. We do actually get a lot of variability here, with the price-to-operating cash flow approach translating to upside of between 6.5% and 62.4%. But when you look at the other two trading multiples, the picture is clear. We get upside of between 79.5% and 130%. This is monumental in the grand scheme of things.

*Excludes Construction Partners for Price/Operating Cash Flow Due to it Being an Outlier

This all assumes that management just maintains the current course. But actually, the upside could be even greater. In the image below, you can see that management has major expectations for the future. They expect to achieve annualized organic revenue growth of between 6% and 8% from 2025 through 2027. They also intend to make between two and three acquisitions each year. This should allow the company to continue improving from a margin standpoint, with a target of an EBITDA margin of between 12.5% and 14.5% by 2027. Even if we see revenue grow a total of 10% annually during this time (from its pro forma figures I calculated for this year), this would mean EBITDA of around $771.8 million by 2027. Even if the firm trades are the same EV to EBITDA multiple as what it does now, and assuming its net leverage scales up to match the same EV to EBITDA multiple it has now on a forward basis, this would still translate to annualized upside of about 14.1%. Considering that the market typically averages 10% to 11% a year, I consider this to be a clear winner.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I have to say that Granite Construction remains a compelling prospect in my book. The company is doing quite well and its recent acquisitions should fuel additional shareholder value creation. Given how cheap the company is on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms, I believe that upgrading it to a ‘strong buy’ makes sense at this point.

data.title: GDP Nowcasts Still Indicate Moderate U.S. Growth For Q3
data.description:
The government shutdown is delaying economic reports, but the latest numbers available continue to indicate a solid increase in the upcoming third quarter GDP report, based on the median estimate for a set of nowcasts compiled by CapitalSpectator.com. The longer the shutdown lasts, however, the greater the uncertainty as the nowcast inputs age and fail to reflect the latest economic changes.

The Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) is scheduled to report its initial estimate of Q3 GDP on October 30–assuming the agency is open. At the moment, BEA is closed due to the government shutdown.

Meanwhile, our median nowcast for Q3 is a solid +2.4%, based on the latest data available. Although that’s down sharply from Q2’s sizzling +3.8%, the current estimate suggests that economic activity was moderately strong during the July through September period. Recession risk, in other words, remained low in the previous quarter.

Warning signs for Q4, however, are emerging. Analysis by Moody’s Analytics, for example, advises that a third of US state economies are contracting and another 13 are “treading water.” Two of the states with the biggest influence on US GDP — California and New York — appear to be at the tipping point. “Those two states are treading water. They’re big states, and if they go into the red, then that’ll probably take the national economy with them into recession,” predicts Moody’s chief economist, Mark Zandi.

Survey data for September indicates that growth downshifted, but remains moderately positive, based on the S&P US Composite PMI, a GDP proxy. The consultancy reports: “Employment meanwhile barely rose, but confidence in the outlook strengthened noticeably. Cost pressures remained elevated, although inflation softened to a five-month low. A similar trend was seen for output charges.”

The longer the shutdown lasts, the bigger the hit for the economy. A rule of thumb from economists is that for every week that the government stays closed, GDP is reduced by 0.1 to 0.2 percentage points.

The government shut down more than a week ago (Oct. 1), and at the moment a political solution still looks elusive. A pair of funding bills to reopen the government were voted down on Thursday in the Senate. Meanwhile, lawmakers showed no sign that they are prepared to back down from their respective party’s positions on funding the government.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Intel: A Great American Turnaround Unfolding Before Your Eyes
data.description:
Since my last Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) analysis, in which I issued a Buy rating, the stock has surged by 50% in price. Since my first Strong Buy rating in June, the stock has risen by 70% in price. I remain bullish, viewing this as America's most pivotal business turnaround in contemporary history.

Since my last analysis of Intel, in which I honed in on a recently inked deal with the U.S. government (which took a 9.9% stake), both Nvidia (NVDA) and Softbank (OTCPK:SFTBY) have come to the table buying approximately a 4% and 2% stake respectively. I believe we are in the early innings of a historical turnaround, which has been a conviction I have had for around six months now and have had a lot of pushback from readers on. However, with respect, I have learned not to listen to commenters usually, as bias is not a reliable indicator of objectivity. In my opinion, (and clearly the opinion of many of the tech industries major players now, and the U.S. government), Intel is a long-term Buy. It should be held through volatility and tolerated amid moments of resistance and fear. This thesis is somewhat speculative if you have to make decisions based on present fundamentals, but the greatest investors often don't make investments based on present fundamentals alone, they also make investments based on thematic trends and a company's probability of high exposure to these trends. Thematic is the nature of how I will present this analysis.

Thematic/Macro Catalyst Horizon

Firstly, Washington has essentially elevated semiconductor sovereignty to the level of defense infrastructure. Intel was already a centerpiece of U.S. government strategy long before the recently declared 9.9% stake. In Intel's 2024 10-K, $7.9 billion in direct CHIPS funding for commercial projects and up to $3 billion under the Secure Enclave program was outlined for trusted manufacturing. The U.S. government purchasing 433.3 million shares at $20.47 each is a natural extension of this (there is also a five-year warrant to purchase an additional 5% at $20 if Intel's ownership of its foundry falls below 51%).

A September 2024 Intel press release confirming the $3 billion Secure Enclave award emphasizes that Intel is the only U.S. company that both designs and manufactures leading-edge logic chips. The program also builds on previous Department of Defense collaborations, including RAMP-C and SHIP. There's a lot of innate alignment here between fiscal and defense policy, which inevitably leads to a strong undercurrent of demand from federal customers. It's natural to infer that in due course Intel's focus may shift somewhat from the consumer domain toward government procurement, though the recent Nvidia deal also specifies an ongoing consumer-related approach, which aligns with Intel's heritage.

Secondly, Intel's long-term resurgence is supported by the global bottleneck in AI hardware. The major fabs, TSMC (TSM) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), are operating near full utilization. Substrate shortages like ABF, HBM, and CoWoS are also further constraining output, even if periodic due to fast capacity ramps. This creates natural demand tailwinds for a new foundry to fulfill burgeoning demand, and Intel is geopolitically suited. The trifecta of high-value players (Washington, Nvidia, and SoftBank) underwriting Intel's turnaround reflects a consensus that Intel's foundry capacity is strategically valuable in supporting (not just a bet that it will work, though that is implicit).

Thirdly, according to the Institute of Progress, global AI data-center power demand could expand by 130 GW by 2030, so the largest long-term barrier to expanding AI capacity in the U.S. is perplexingly not chip supply but power availability. Intel's Ohio site is positioned for $100B in investment over the next decade (initial expenditures are $28B), and the site is also preparing for long-term purchase power agreements. Fabs that are anchored by stable, clean power are going to be sovereign assets in the future, and Intel is positioning now to reap these benefits through a structural moat invested in way ahead of time. It will take many years (but the best things always do). Basically, as energy and semiconductor alpha merges, Intel's early capex commitments will be viewed by the market as a strategic hedge against the volatility of pure-play AI stocks.

Probabilistic Return Framework

In the bull case whereby Intel successfully executes its 18A and 14A process roadmap in Intel Foundry, the company will likely secure significant contracts from hyperscalers. As time progresses, scaled customers could logically include Nvidia, Microsoft (MSFT), and Amazon (AMZN). Under this scenario, it's conceivable to me that the re-rating of the company's valuation alongside its positive revisions in growth estimates from analysts will lead to a 30%+ price CAGR. This is in fact the thesis I am currently weighting as most probable, even though it currently looks difficult to imagine other than being substantiated by early allocations from the U.S. government, Nvidia, and SoftBank.

There is then the base case which is perhaps the most rational outlook to allocate from. Policy funding would remain in place, but there would be meaningful administrative delays or partial rollbacks. Macroeconomically, Intel's rise in the foundry market could coincide with a drawdown in capital expenditures on AI hardware, leading to more general tech valuation re-ratings and making Intel's scale-up poorly timed. Still, even in this outcome due to the present durability of AI demand and the breadth of penetration, Intel's reasonable value at the moment (forward P/S of 3.34 that is -4.84% below the sector median) and strong long-term growth optionality, indicates a steady CAGR of 15–20% in a lackluster normal growth outcome.

The positive asymmetry of this investment exists in the fact that in the bear case Intel would need to exhibit ongoing operational failure like process yields on 18A/14A stagnating, CHIPS funding being reduced amid fiscal tightening or political turnover, and AI hardware demand significantly plateauing. Every capital spending cycle has to mature, and it's conceivable that AI growth will plateau at the macro level by 2028 (which is an opinion of mine based on my extensive ongoing analysis of present macro and capex trends; please ask in the comments if you'd like more information). Intel would probably have to revert to its PC and server segments and its narrative value would collapse, leaving investors with something akin to a -10% compound annual contraction. To me, this is a tail risk, as recent ownership from Washington, Nvidia, and SoftBank should provide enough soft direction alongside the leadership of turnaround specialist CEO Lip-Bu Tan to make the company's endeavors a probable success.

I have approximately weighted these outcomes as 50% bull-case probable, 30% base-case probable, and 20% bear-case probable. My expected annual return over the next few years for Intel is about 22.5% if buying at about $35. I will be seeking to accumulate at ~$32.5 or lower if such a price arises, given that I seek a 30% reliable CAGR typically.

Conclusion: Buy

Intel may not be a stock for those who don't like to visualize future outcomes that are not presently tangible, because it requires a modicum of this to be successful in this allocation. That said, for those who can see clearly that the backing of three major players (Washington, Nvidia, and SoftBank), means something significant, accumulating shares at a reasonable price after a retracement from present euphoria is not only clever, it is probabilistically likely to generate high alpha. Intel is a textbook value turnaround investment, and I believe it will be looked back on in the years to come as one of America's greatest contemporary business stories.

data.title: GitLab: 20% Top-Line Growth Powered By Explosion In Generative AI Code
data.description:
GitLab (NASDAQ:GTLB) is an example of a high-growth software name which is still trading at reasonable valuations in spite of an overheated tech market. Investors appear concerned about potential disruption from generative AI, even if management has the opposite view. The company maintains a strong balance sheet and I expect it to achieve GAAP profitability over the coming quarters. I find the valuation more than reasonable given the aggressive forward growth rates. I am initiating coverage with a buy rating.

GTLB Stock Price

GTLB was one of the pandemic tech favorites and has not yet returned to 2021 highs. While I am bullish on the stock, I acknowledge that it might take some time to make that return given the excessive valuations with which it had traded at.

GTLB Stock Key Metrics

GTLB is an enterprise software company known for DevOps capabilities, including version control, pipeline, integration, and other products. If that didn't make sense, GTLB helps software customers create software. GTLB has seen its valuation multiples compress as investors appear to be questioning if the company will be a beneficiary of generative AI (due to more code to maintain) or a victim (perhaps due to DevOps competing products created by generative AI).

In its most recent quarter, GTLB generated 29% YoY revenue growth to $236 million, exceeding guidance of between $226 million to $227 million.

The company continues to see solid customer growth although at a decelerating pace due to scale.

On the profitability front, the company delivered 700 bps of non-GAAP operating margin expansion, crushing guidance for 10.6% margins.

I note that the company has also made progress on a GAAP basis, narrowing the GAAP operating loss to $18.4 million and (after accounting for interest income) is now within striking distance of breakeven GAAP net income. I would not be surprised if GTLB is able to break into sustained GAAP profitability (at least on a net income basis) as soon as the next fiscal year.

GTLB ended the quarter with $1.2 billion of cash versus no debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management guided the third quarter to see up to $239 million in revenue, representing 21.9% YoY growth, representing a steep sequential deceleration in growth rates. Management barely increased the full-year guide which now implies around $252.5 million of fourth quarter revenue, representing a 19.4% YoY growth rate exiting the year.

I suspect that there is significant conservatism baked into the guidance in part due to the fact that Brian Robins would be stepping down as CFO in order to join Snowflake (SNOW) at the same post. On the conference call, management also noted "incremental softness in SMB" that they expect for the rest of the year. Management also highlighted "Agentic partnerships with Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Amazon and Cursor" which underscores the company's positioning to benefit from ongoing advances in code development. Management noted that "over 70% of our revenue growth in FY '26 is due to paid seat growth," a unique disclosure that I suspect was ironically received negatively from investors who have been fretting about the potential generative AI disruption to seat-based vendors (in blunt terms, the potential for mass layoffs). Management paired this disclosure with the note that "less than 10% of the FY '26 revenue growth was derived from the premium price increase." Unfortunately, I am not sure if investors will focus on that part. Management believes that generative AI will be a tailwind for their company, noting that code generation "accounts for about 20% of a developer's time, while 80% of the time is really managing change of that code across the software life cycle."

Is GTLB Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, GTLB traded hands at just around 8x forward sales. Net cash made up around 14% of the market cap but I will exclude this from valuation calculations for conservatism.

The company has not yet achieved GAAP profitability but I expect net margins to head towards 30% over the long term. I base that assumption on both the high 88% gross margin as well as where profit margins stand at mature software vendors like Microsoft (MSFT) and Adobe (ADBE). That places the current valuation at around 27x long term earnings. I expect the company to sustain around 20% top-line growth at least over the next couple of years. That places the price to earnings growth ratio ('PEG ratio') at around 1.4x (I note that I use the top-line growth for the 'G' variable for sustainability). I view that PEG ratio to be quite reasonable. Another way to look at it - as GTLB matures into a highly profitable cash machine, it would not be out of the question for the stock to still command an 8x sales multiple (and 27x earnings multiple). I can see the stock sustaining the current revenue multiple, implying that the stock may be able to deliver annual upside in-line with top-line growth. Assuming top-line growth stays in the 15% to 20% range over the near to medium future, that should be enough to allow for market-beating returns.

GTLB Stock Risks

GTLB faces stiff competition from Microsoft's GitHub and others. It is possible that MSFT's generative AI capabilities allow it to offer superior product offerings in the future. It is possible that generative AI leads to a different coding environment in which GTLB's products are no longer relevant. It is possible that generative AI allows customers to build devOps products in-house. It is possible that generative AI leads to mass layoffs which would hurt GTLB's seat-based growth. GTLB is not yet generating GAAP profits and thus may face elevated volatility in the event of a market downturn.

GTLB Stock Conclusion

GTLB's underperformance over the past many quarters has given investors an attractive opportunity to purchase shares of a growth compounder at reasonable valuations. Management's outlook for the rest of the year may have spooked investors but I suspect that they are merely adding conservatism upon which they can beat later. The company appears set to break into GAAP profitability in the upcoming fiscal year which alongside the net cash balance sheet would bolster the financial safety profile. GTLB shares many of the traits I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am initiating coverage with a buy rating.

data.title: AMD: OpenAI Got A Bargin - I Wouldn't Hold Into Earnings
data.description:
OpenAI is on a spending spree, and the latest on its hit list is Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD), which popped as much as 34% Monday on news that OpenAI will purchase tens of billions of dollars’ worth of chips with a total power consumption of 6 GWs, and potentially take a 10% stake in the company over time. The stock continued to gain on Tuesday and Wednesday and closed only slightly lower by 1% on Thursday.

The left number shows AMD's stock on Monday, while the latter shows its stock on Wednesday. Since the market opened, the stock is up another 11% to $235.56, breaking all-time highs.

Smart money will be asking lots of questions about this and not wasting any time celebrating a rally that may not have legs until 2H25. For starters, what’s the total dollar figure on this transaction? The answer is basically the fact that OpenAI will be able to take a 10% stake in AMD at the very low cost of $0.01 per share and, in turn, buy AMD's MI450s. AMD's press release read as follows:

As part of the agreement, to further align strategic interests, AMD has issued OpenAI a warrant for up to 160 million shares of AMD common stock, structured to vest as specific milestones are achieved. The first tranche vests with the initial 1 gigawatt deployment, with additional tranches vesting as purchases scale up to 6 gigawatts.

It looks like OpenAI just got a hell of a deal because what we know is that OpenAI executives assume that 1GW = $50B to bring online, and about two-thirds of that cost goes into the chips themselves and infrastructure around them. So, if OpenAI is looking to purchase 6GW, that would translate to around $200B. Instead, OpenAI has $100B from Nvidia and now has a 6GW deployment at negative cost. In fact, if you assume they already bought those shares, they would've seen a substantial return. So, while the overall narrative is growing more cautious of OpenAI's deal-making, which the Financial Times estimates to top $1T, we don't think the AMD deal should be a point of concern for now.

There will be a time to worry about OpenAI, but we don't think there is enough cause to worry about AMD yet. If you add OpenAI’s commitments, they add up to $332B (this rivals the current AI CapEx spend from all tier 1 players, including Meta Platforms (META), Google (GOOGL) Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT), combined this year, which is at $330B). As of last week, OpenAI is the highest valued start-up, now valued at $500B, and its high spending in relation to its extremely modest revenue is a reason for concern. OpenAI generated $4.3B in revenue during 1H25, according to the Information report from the end of September. While this is 16% more than the same period last year, it’s still not the kind of money that would make headlines with every Big Tech company out there. OpenAI stated that its current cash burn rate is $2.5 billion, primarily due to high R&D expenses. The FY25 target is revenue of $13B and a cash burn target of $8.5B. Concerns over circular structure/financing are high with so many AI infrastructure deals going around. And we're usually the first in line to get wary of too much spending in one direction. But we think this deal is actually the exception because OpenAI is getting a bargain and AMD is getting back in on the AI story. It's a win-win.

Why does this matter for AMD?

A big part of why the stock is rallying is that it was so hammered down heading into this print. Remember that AMD stock took a ~9% nosedive after reporting Q2 results and guiding for Q3 sales without China AI chips. The stock was stuck in a flattish action heading into this news, down about 1% between August 19th and Monday morning. The stock actually only grew 12% during the calendar year Q3, pretty much in line with the SOX benchmark, which was up about 15%. AMD stock is now the top outperformer within our semi coverage QTD, up about 45%.

We upgraded the stock after it hit the $160 range; since then, it has been up 31%. We think that AMD needed a win and got one that the market was hungry enough to eat up as a massive positive.

AMD CEO, Lisa Su, got on Bloomberg this Monday with the president of OpenAI, Greg Brockman, and mentioned the $500B TAM in AI accelerators over the next five years, and called this estimate conservative from current visibility. The deal will commit OpenAI to purchasing AMD’s next-gen MI450. The AMD MI450 competes directly with Nvidia’s (NVDA) Blackwell series, but won’t be out until 2H26, and the deal makes OpenAI the biggest customer for AMD from current visibility. We don't expect to see any financial tailwinds from this deal until 2H26. This is why we think it's dangerous to own the stock in print. Expectations are running hot with 27 upward EPS revisions and 41 upward revenue revisions over the past three months.

FYI, OpenAI isn’t just now purchasing from AMD; in fact, back when AMD held its AI event in mid-June, OpenAI’s Sam Altman was not only there but also seemingly star-struck with the lineup. Altman said, “When you first started telling me about the specs, I was like, there’s no way, that just sounds totally crazy…' It’s gonna be an amazing thing.'" A lot of the positives are priced in at current levels, but we do see more upside ahead for the stock after getting looped back into the AI story.

RSI is now through the roof at 81. The stock itself has been hovering below overbought territory since earnings, because that is when the market started to lose confidence in AMD’s growth story. The stock, in fact, underperformed the S&P 500 benchmark during Q3 as mentioned above. We do expect a cool down after this week's run-up, with the stock up almost 40% over the past five days, bringing AMD's market cap up to $381.74B. We see any cool down as a buying opportunity.

Risks heading for 3Q25

For 3Q25, AMD is guiding for revenue of $8.7B at the midpoint, and analyst expectations are for $8.73B. The big concern is that there is lackluster fuel behind AMD’s AI rally, and that what is working out well for them, which is their CPU (server and client) business line, could be under threat now that Nvidia is partnering up with their nemesis Intel (INTC). This could allow Intel to maintain its share of the CPU market and potentially even gain some of the share it has bled to AMD back. According to Digitimes, Intel has already planned to increase its pricing on Raptor Lake processors by +10%.

The other concern is AMD’s gross margins, which got them a downgrade from Erste Group, due to their being lower than the sector average. Last quarter, AMD’s non-GAAP gross margin contracted 1,040 basis points sequentially to 43.3%. Now, that decline was due to the Chinese inventory write-off. But, if we look back a quarter, AMD’s gross margins were still under pressure, having contracted 40 basis points due to lower sales. Next-gen MI3450 could boost margins, enabling a higher ASP, but we don't see margin tailwinds until then.

These concerns were priced into the stock price before Monday’s news, but now expectations are once again getting hot. We see risk of a potential pullback post-Q3 print, similar to what we saw post-Q2 earnings. Our take is that the market reaction will depend on its broader sentiment towards tech in early November, after we've gained some visibility from others reporting earlier in the quarter, such as Intel, for example. We'll update our coverage on AMD pre-earnings depending on the information this earnings season brings.

The real risk heading toward the 3Q25 earnings call is that AMD's stock price performance post-print has a bad track record for this year. So, this is a risk investors should be aware of heading into November, and one that can be mitigated by having some extra dry powder to buy any dramatic market reaction. With that in mind, we strongly believe AMD stock has more upside until Q3 prints more, especially so after the stock broke $227, which was its all-time high. Hence, we maintain our buy rating on the stock as we see more upside to AMD's growth story in light of the strategic OpenAI deal.

data.title: Athabasca Oil: Cyclical Peak May Be Near
data.description:
Athabasca Oil Corporation (OTCPK:ATHOF) has been doing rather well lately. This is likely due to the startup of the Trans Mountain Expansion Pipeline which began operations in May 2024. The last article covered this. This startup allowed a fair amount of heavy oil and thermal oil to be shipped to market. It therefore firmed up the discount to light oil for the time being. But any time profits increase, it is to the advantage of each company to increase production which will likely bring the larger discount back eventually. There is talk about another pipeline to get even more product available to the export market. But talk is far from appropriate action. It is very likely that the current situation is near the top of the business cycle. That makes this company a sell consideration.

There is also the risk that the discount will widen during the next cyclical downturn to the point where heavy oil or thermal production may need to be shut-in until there is a cyclical recovery. However, that would severely decrease the cash flow available to this heavy oil producer.

Cash Strategy

There has long been an unusual cash strategy at work here that is likely a concession to the realities of the thermal business. Athabasca itself is a thermal producer.

As shown above, the company keeps a fair amount of cash on hand for the size of the company while also keeping a fairly large debt balance. Personally, I feel it is much better for investors to repay that debt while keeping the desirable amount of cash on hand. While that is still likely to be a sizable balance, at least there is no debt to worry about in the event of a cyclical downturn.

Note that the cash flow available easily handles the debt load during times like the present. But heavy oil and thermal oil producers often have larger losses than their unconventional competitors due to a widening discount during downturns. That often makes any debt load a challenge. It has more than once threatened the viability of one of these producers due to the widening discount that often produced negative cash flow.

For this reason, the cyclical high point earnings are not as valued because the downcycle can be a lot lower. Overall earnings are more volatile in this business than is the case for traditional unconventional. But that usually means it is rare for a thermal oil producer to survive long-term as an independent without acquiring some refining capacity to avoid the widening discount market issues during a cyclical downturn.

Light Oil

This company has made several attempts at joint ventures for light oil production. Shown below is the latest idea:

This is an attempt to get significant cash flow from a premium product. This joint venture with Cenovus has the potential to add enough cash flow from a premium product production so that there would be no worries during a cyclical downturn.

The warning here is that the company has tried this before without much success. Whether this joint venture succeeds where the others failed is up for discussion.

In the meantime, the startup looks pretty good, and there appear to be some promising results. The ability to get to the targeted 20,000 BOED is probably critical to reducing the risk of insufficient cash flow during a cyclical downturn.

This is definitely a way to have thermal oil production while surviving the likely severe results from the discount widening during the more hostile part of the business cycle. But there has to be enough light oil cash flow to make this a viable strategy for the company. No partnership has done that so far.

Thermal Oil Projects

In the meantime, this company like several in the industry has plans to increase thermal oil production. Profits are excellent at the current time. But that means that the current situation most likely signals a business cycle peak for heavy oil and thermal oil production.

Most of the thermal oil producers are now taking advantage of the great cash flow and lower discount to increase production. However, it is basic cyclical industry theory that the production increases coming online will likely not only bring back a typical discount to light oil, but also production will "go past" that market to likely bring about an excess of heavy oil and thermal oil to produce a cyclical bottom yet again.

Stock Price

The stock price appears to be at a multiyear high. However, that likely means that both heavy oil producers and thermal oil producers never had it so good.

Whenever I see a chart like the above for a notoriously cyclical industry, it is a primary indication to do some work because it may be time to take profits. I have no clue how high the stock will go.

But the key is always to avoid big losses. The old Wall Street saying is about "Sometimes the bears make money. Sometimes the bulls make money. But the pigs never make money." applies. If one has been bullish about the company, then the time to take profits is when the good times appear to be around infinitely into the future. That time is now.

Summary

As has been the case for some time, now is the time to take profits in heavy oil and thermal oil producers. That is especially true for a company like this one with substantial outstanding debt.

This company has so far survived. But that does not take away from the fact that most heavy oil and thermal oil producers do not stay independent long-term unless they acquire refining assets.

The threat of a widening discount during a cyclical downturn makes this a sell consideration. A widening discount can make the overall sales price unfavorable even if light oil is cash flowing sufficiently during the cyclical downturn.

That makes this idea an unacceptable risk for most investors. At the very least, I would recover my investment cost plus a reasonable profit to only allow "house money" for any remaining investment. Frankly at this point, I think a lottery ticket offers better future odds.

The good times can last for a while. But that does not mean the investment should remain a viable idea for a while.

Risks

Another pipeline to Canada's West Coast could possibly extend the good times for some time to come. However, that is in the talking stage as opposed to the construction stage. Therefore, such talk is really just idle conversation until proven otherwise.

Some sort of unforeseen shortage similar to 2022 could likewise extend the good times or even make them better. Right now, there is nothing like that on the horizon.

Personally, I prefer companies in this business to have no debt. That at least reduces the financial risk during a cyclical downturn. Since it does have substantial long-term debt (and it appears to have no immediate plans to repay that debt), I would look for better opportunities elsewhere at the current stock price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BW LPG Could Support A Double-Digit Yield Even With Rates Under Pressure
data.description:
I have covered several companies in the shipping industry over the years, but BW LPG Limited (NYSE:BWLP) never managed to draw my attention. But its case appears quite interesting to me today because very large gas carrier rates have cooled, and the stock has followed suit. Investors are probably wondering if the huge dividends they saw in the previous two years will ever return. And yet, it seems that even with rates under pressure, the math still works, and a rebound wouldn’t need much to push the yield to the double digits.

The Dividend Setup

BW LPG has historically paid massive dividends. Its dividend policy changes in line with leverage, with a payout of 50–100% of shipping NPAT. In any case, dividends end up being quite high even during sub-optimal market conditions. To its credit, since its IPO, the company has paid out about 3/4 of its earnings as dividends.

BW Group, BWLP's holding company, is the largest owner, holding roughly 30% of the shares. Their incentives have therefore historically aligned with common holders income-wise.

Impressively, in the up-cycle (2023), BWLP paid $3.46/share in dividends. That was a 23% annualized yield off the period-end share price. Last year, it still paid a hefty $2.42/share even as earnings declined, marking a 21% annualized yield off the year-end share price.

This year, the market’s softer rates are evident in the gradual drop of quarterly payouts. They came in at $0.28 for Q1 and an even lower $0.22 for Q2, on EPS of $0.30 and $0.23, respectively.

Rates Are Softer, But The Math Still Works

Last quarter, BW LPG posted VLGC TCE income of $38,800/available day, down from $49,700/day the year before, which, as I just mentioned, made its mark in the underlying hit to EPS and DPS. Note that the company did say decent time-charter cover supporting earnings, but the overall direction QoQ has been down.

You can see how fast earnings react when the U.S.–Far East LPG arbitrage narrows (less cargo margin to share with shipping) or Panama Canal dynamics shift. When that arb narrowed earlier in the year, spot VLGC rates came under pressure. If they widen again, ton-mile demand and rates should go back up.

Now, where does that leave us today? Well, annualizing the most recent $0.22 quarterly dividend against BWLP’s ~$13 share price implies a forward yield around the mid-6% range. That's nowhere near the monster yields from a couple of years ago.

However, as you can see in BW LPG's latest presentation, utilizing this year's fixed-out positions and a cash breakeven in the mid-$24,000s/day, the company could sustain a high single-digit yield of around $50k/day and a low double-digit yield of about 11%, around $60k/day. I should mention that the stock price management used at the time the graph was made (as per the presentation's notes) was around $12.50 (in Aug 28). Therefore, a double-digit yield at today’s levels seems possible, even close to $50k/day, which is around current levels.

And, if rates were to start to move back up, a yield above 15%-20% at today's share price cost shouldn't be that tough to achieve. Continued U.S. export growth, with capacity additions from the second half of this year, could support volumes. In turn, vessel demand could be a positive driver for rates. And clearly, more barrels and longer voyages should lead to higher TCEs. Also, note that the widening between BLPG3 (U.S.–Japan) and BLPG1 (MEG–Japan) as U.S. policy changes push vessel redeployments. This may also tighten the effective supply and support rates.

What Could Go Wrong, And What It’s Worth

What I fear the most about BWLP's case is that VLGC order book because it sits north of 100 ships (with 2027 being particularly heavy), and deliveries can cap rallies if demand doesn’t keep the same or faster pace. Moreover, if the U.S.–Asia arb stays tight, we might see a repeat of the Q2 pattern, with healthy utilization but then again less pricing power.

Valuation-wise, I don't have much to comment on. BWL is essentially trading close to its book value. Whether you believe it should trade at 0.9x, 1.1x, or 1.2x has to do with your personal projection of how high or low rates (and thus, earnings) can move from here.

I, personally, feel that rates will find support at today's levels and could even move higher if the order book setup doesn't eat much of the incoming growth in demand. Along with the potential for a double-digit dividend yield at current rates or modestly higher rates, I view BWLP as more or less fairly valued.

Final Thoughts

Look, rates are softer, and the recent trend in dividends reflects that. Still, BWLP benefits from a low cash break-even point. The very clear payout policy is also a plus, and I don't see its largest shareholder stopping from continuing to prioritize hefty payouts over the long run.

At today’s price, the forward yield sits around 6.7% based on the most recent quarterly payment. Then again, BWLP's sensitivity chart shows potential for a double-digit yield at today's share price, especially if TCE climbs back into the $60k/day area. And with seasonal tailwinds and structural export growth, I like the odds that the distribution trend goes up from here.
